runs.interpretation: Intertemporal elasticity of substitution (IES) for nondurable consumption in annual macro calibration, measuring the percent change in nondurable consumption growth rate from a 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption, distinct from risk aversion.
citations: Hall (1988) on consumption and income growth
Campbell and Mankiw (1989) on permanent income hypothesis tests
Lustig and Van Nieuwerburgh (2008) on consumption inequality and IES estimation
runs.reasoningSummary: The IES for nondurable consumption is a central parameter in macro models. Empirical estimates from macro and microeconomic studies cluster around 0.5, though estimates vary considerably (roughly 0.2-1.5 depending on methodology). The distinction from risk aversion (captured separately via standard utility curvature) is critical. Annual calibrations typically use values in the 0.4-0.6 range based on long-run consumption growth responses to interest rate changes. Lower bounds reflect estimation difficulties and preference for conservative calibration; higher values reflect some structural estimation evidence but remain less commonly used in standard models.

Empirical estimates from macro and microeconomic studies cluster around 0.5, though estimates vary considerably (roughly 0.2-1.5 depending on methodology). The distinction from risk aversion (captured separately via standard utility curvature) is critical. Annual calibrations typically use values in the 0.4-0.6 range based on long-run consumption growth responses to interest rate changes. Lower bounds reflect estimation difficulties and preference for conservative calibration; higher values reflect some structural estimation evidence but remain less commonly used in standard models."}

runs.interpretation: Intertemporal elasticity of substitution (IES) for nondurable consumption in annual macro-calibration, measuring the percent change in consumption growth rate from a 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption, distinct from risk aversion.
citations: Hall (1988) on consumption and intertemporal substitution
Attanasio and Weber (2010) survey on consumption elasticities
Prescott (1986) on macro calibration standards
runs.reasoningSummary: The IES for nondurable consumption is empirically contentious. Macro calibrations often target values between 0.5-1.0, reflecting modest intertemporal substitution. Hall's influential work and subsequent labor supply evidence suggest values closer to 0.5, while some preference specifications allow higher values. I center on 0.5 with a 90% interval spanning 0.25-1.0 to reflect ongoing empirical uncertainty and heterogeneity across estimation methods.

runs.interpretation: IES for nondurable consumption in annual macro calibration, distinct from risk aversion
citations: Hall (1988) on intertemporal substitution elasticity
Mehra and Prescott (1985) equity premium puzzle
Barro (2006) on macroeconomic elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Macro evidence suggests modest IES for nondurable consumption, typically in the 0.3-0.7 range for annual calibrations. Hall's foundational work showed limited evidence for high substitution elasticity. Mehra-Prescott and subsequent research on the equity premium generally support lower IES values. Most modern macro models use values between 0.5-1.0, with 0.5 being a common benchmark. Lower estimates reflect weak empirical evidence for large consumption responses to intertemporal price changes.

citations: Hall (1988) - Life-cycle permanent income hypothesis and IES estimates

runs.reasoningSummary: Standard macro calibrations typically employ IES estimates in the range 0.4-0.6 for nondurable consumption. This reflects empirical difficulty in precisely pinning down IES separately from risk aversion. Hall's landmark work suggested IES near 0, while subsequent research has pushed estimates higher. Lower values (0.25-0.4) reflect pessimistic empirical findings; higher values (0.7-1.0) reflect some theoretical preferences and certain empirical approaches.

Vissing-Jørgensen and Attanasio (2003) - Stock-market participation, intertemporal substitution, and risk-aversion
runs.reasoningSummary: The IES for nondurable consumption has been extensively studied in macro and asset pricing. Hall (1988) and subsequent work suggest values substantially below 1, with much evidence pointing toward 0.3-0.8 range for aggregate nondurable consumption. Annual calibrations typically use values around 0.5, reflecting moderate intertemporal substitution and empirical estimates from consumption growth and interest rate dynamics. runs.interpretation: IES for nondurable consumption in annual macro-calibration settings, measuring the percent change in consumption growth rate from a 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption, distinct from risk aversion
citations: Hall (1988) - intertemporal substitution elasticity
Campbell and Mankiw (1989) - consumption dynamics
Attanasio and Weber (2010) - consumption and macro calibration
runs.reasoningSummary: The IES is empirically challenging to estimate and estimates vary widely. Micro evidence often suggests values below 1, frequently in the 0.5-0.7 range for nondurable consumption. Macro calibrations commonly use 0.5 as a baseline, though sensitivity analyses span 0.2-1.0. My credence centers around 0.5 with substantial uncertainty reflecting the persistent disagreement in the literature between microeconomic and macroeconomic approaches.
